ABB Migration.notes:
## v4

Reworked around what a migration actually needs — a tag list, an IO list and a
cross-reference — rather than a dump of ABB's internal structures.

**Three joined deliverables** are now written to the output root, with an
`index.html` to browse everything:

- **`master_tag_list.csv`** — every plant tag with its class, description, plant
  area, controller, scan task and scan rate, engineering range and units, alarm
  limits with hysteresis and enables, OPC/trend exposure, and the programs and
  screens that use it.
- **`io_list.csv`** — every IO channel with its controller, rack/module path,
  `module.channel` address, the signal wired to it, that signal's description, and
  the tagged block that consumes it. Channels with no signal are your spares.
- **`signal_xref.csv`** — every signal: type, description, owning controller, IO
  address, which programs write it and which read it, which screens display it,
  and whether it is used *outside* its owning controller.

**Data that earlier versions dropped entirely** is now extracted: the master tag
list (7.3k tags with descriptions), the signal dictionary (9.9k signals), the
signal-to-controller cross-reference, OPC/trend exposure, plant areas, enum/state
texts, task definitions, peer-to-peer telegrams, the user-defined function-block
library, and the project's real HMI colour palette.

**Control logic** is readable at last. Wire endpoints previously could not be
resolved to a block pin at all; they now resolve **100%**, producing a real FBD
netlist in `extracted_lad/logic_connections.csv`.

**Graphics** now export the process tag behind every element — which tag drives
which graphic, and how (display / animate / write / symbol) — plus faceplate
targets and the screen navigation graph. Rendered screens gained pipework, symbols
and the correct colours, and you can hover any element to see its tag.

### Fixes

- The hardware section was silently truncated to a **single node**; all 432 now
  extract as a proper tree, and IO channels carry their controller and rack path.
- The two most valuable IO columns were emitted as `col6` / `col7`. They are the
  assigned plant signal and the `module.channel` address, and are now named.
- Most graphic symbols had no definition, because the macro name was read from the
  wrong field. All 56 macros now extract.
- `parameters.csv` in v3 held ~19k rows of misrouted data; the section it claimed
  to read is empty in a real export. The engineering values it advertised are the
  block parameters, now surfaced in the tag list with a legend for ABB's German
  mnemonics (`Mba`/`Mbe` = range, `Gw` = alarm limit, …).
- Re-running into an existing output folder silently **doubled every table**.

SuperOPCWatchIt.notes:
# SuperOPCWatchIt — release notes

## v3

**Standalone OPC-DA — nothing to install, no "Class not registered".**

- Replaced the OpenOPC + `OPC.Automation` wrapper stack with a vendored native
  32-bit helper (`opcda-bridge.exe`) that talks the OPC-DA custom interfaces
  directly. The tool no longer needs any OPC automation wrapper (Graybox /
  Matrikon / `gbda_aut.dll`) or any COM registration — the only requirement is
  the OPC server itself (e.g. `OPC.DeltaV.1`), which is already present on a
  DeltaV node.
- Fixes the "Connection error / Dispatch: Class not registered" failure, which
  was caused by the missing automation-wrapper COM class.
- Clearer connection errors: the status line now reports the actual OPC/COM
  reason (e.g. server ProgID not registered, item not writeable, unknown item).

*Note:* runs on the DeltaV node itself (local COM). Connecting to a remote
node's OPC server over DCOM is not enabled in this build.

## v2

- Advanced OPC-DA tag monitor: live browsing, CSV import/export, write audit log.

DifFHX.notes:
## v9

- Fixed very large FHX files appearing to hang while loading. The step that learns how
  to name elements uniquely searched every combination of up to five attributes, which
  on a wide export ran to hundreds of millions of attempts and never completed. Because
  DifFHX learns one naming rule across both files being compared, it was more exposed to
  this than the single-file tools. Files that previously would not load now finish in
  seconds.

FHXport.notes:
## v22

Historian Report — from feedback by Leonard Clark.

- **Unit modules are no longer missing.** Modules with history collection configured
  on them were silently absent from the report. On a real plant export this recovered
  10 collection points that had never appeared.
- **Controller nodes are now included too** — found while fixing the above, and a
  bigger gap: 72 more points on the same export, including controller scan-time
  loading and free memory.
- **The report is now an Excel workbook** with a sheet each for Modules, Unit Modules
  and Controller Nodes, instead of one flat CSV. Note the output file is now
  `<name>_HistorianReport.xlsx` — if you have a script picking up the `.csv` by name,
  it needs updating.
- **Column changes**, to line the report up with the standard DeltaV historian export:
  - `VALUE_RECORDED` (e.g. `LAS-36501A/PV.CV`) replaces the separate `PARAMETER`
    and `FIELD` columns
  - `TAG` is now `MODULE_NAME`, `UNIT_NAME` or `NODE_NAME`, per sheet
  - `EXPOSED` is now `ALLOW_INSTANCE_CONFIG`
  - added `MODULE_TYPE` and `MODULE_SUBTYPE`

Also in this release: Named Set Reports, and an SFC formatting fix in the EM and
Phase reports.

FHXEdit.notes:
## v8

- Fixed very large FHX exports appearing to hang after parsing. The log would report
  "Successfully parsed N top-level elements" and then go quiet, with Open Editor View,
  Split, Trim and Update Timestamps staying greyed out indefinitely. Parsing had not
  actually finished: a post-parse step that learns how to name elements uniquely was
  searching every combination of up to five attributes, which on a wide file meant
  hundreds of millions of attempts. A 331 MB customer export now loads in about 35
  seconds; previously it would not finish at all.
- The status log now reports progress through that identity-learning step, so it can
  no longer look finished while work is still running.
- Fixed unreadable text on machines with Windows dark mode enabled. The tree filter
  box, element rows in the tree, the Advanced Find/Replace window and the parse
  confirmation dialog could all render text the same colour as their background.

FHXMerge.notes:
## v3

- Fixed very large FHX files appearing to hang while loading. The step that learns how
  to name elements uniquely searched every combination of up to five attributes, which
  on a wide export ran to hundreds of millions of attempts and never completed. Files
  that previously would not load now finish in seconds.
- Fixed unreadable text on machines with Windows dark mode enabled — the merge tree,
  the decision counter and filter button in the header, the diff panes, the Advanced
  Find/Replace window and message dialogs could all render text the same colour as
  their background.

DvTwin.notes:
## v19

- Fixed unreadable text on machines with Windows dark mode enabled. Test-run status
  rows were the worst affected — a coloured status background was applied without a
  matching text colour, so some rows rendered white text on a white background. The
  historian configuration dialog's database path label could also disappear. The app
  now uses a consistent light theme regardless of the Windows theme.

## v18

- Fixed a startup crash that prevented the app from launching. After the shared FHX parser was consolidated, the FHX simulation generator constructed parser objects with an incompatible constructor, raising `TypeError: Element.__init__() got an unexpected keyword argument 'classifier'` at import time. The shared `Element` now accepts those keyword arguments and the app launches again.

VMTools.notes:
## v4

- Fixed hard-to-read text on machines with Windows dark mode enabled. The selected-user
  label on the Local Users tab and the Hyper-V help dialog rendered dark blue on a dark
  background; the app now uses a consistent light theme regardless of the Windows theme.
- Version number brought back in line with the launcher (the app reported v2 while the
  launcher listed v3).

ABComms.notes:
## v4

- Restored the EDS-based chassis catalog lookup. The shared Allen-Bradley EDS library was missing from the packaged build, so catalog-number resolution silently failed; it is now bundled and working again.

P&ID Reader.notes:
# P&ID Reader

## v4 — 3D View

- **New "3D View" step.** Unfold a processed drawing into a navigable 3D
  process-flow diagram that opens in your web browser.
  - Devices are laid out on a clean grid: process equipment (vessels, pumps,
    valves) on the ground, instruments floating on a signal layer above.
  - Pipes and instrument leads are routed with right angles only (no diagonals),
    with flow-direction arrows where the drawing shows them.
  - **Click a device to isolate it** — everything except that device and its
    connections dims out, so you can trace a line at a glance.
  - **Top-down (2D) mode** for a clean schematic overhead view, plus toggles for
    labels, signal lines, the instrument deck, and low-confidence "unclassified"
    shapes (hidden by default).
  - Hover any device for its tag, type, grid cell, loop and confidence.
- **Reflects your review.** The 3D view is generated from the same reviewed
  record as the rest of the tool, so tag corrections (Review step) and symbol
  group labels (Symbols step) carry straight through — shapes you mark
  "Not a symbol" are dropped from the view.

It's a schematic reading and tracing aid built from the extracted graph — not a
physical plant model (a P&ID has no real dimensions or elevations).

Op2Live.notes:
# Op2Live

## v21
- **Faster conversions, identical results.** Building the auto-generated GEM library and
  applying the XSLT conversion were re-reading and re-writing each file many times over. Both
  now share a single in-memory pass per file: the GEM library step makes **~7× fewer passes**
  over each class (about **2× faster** on a full project's worth of GEMs, measured on real
  plant data), and display conversion re-parses each file far less. The converted output is
  **byte-for-byte the same** — this is purely a speed-up, verified against the previous version
  on a real project.
- **No more silently-blank displays on a memory-starved PC.** On a machine very low on free
  memory, the graphics engine could run out of memory *while writing* a converted display,
  leave it **blank**, and still count it as converted — so a blank picture could slip through
  unnoticed. That blank/truncated output is now detected and the display is **automatically
  retried** (a second, lower-pressure pass usually completes it); if it still can't be produced
  it is **clearly reported** rather than shipped empty. Closing other apps (or converting on a
  box with more RAM) remains the real fix for very large displays on tight memory.

## v20
- **GEM libraries load from a whole export tree — and from a `.zip`.** Adding a DeltaV Live
  library export (e.g. a `PCSD V14` folder) used to load **zero** classes: the scanner only
  looked one folder deep, but exports nest every `.gc.ahc` two or three folders down
  (`GEM Classes/PCSD/Components_CD/…`). The scanner is now recursive, so pointing at the export
  root loads the entire library in one go. You can also **import a zipped library** directly:
  it's extracted (nested zips unpacked too) and scanned recursively — no manual unzip.
- **Out-of-box GEMs are flagged, not guessed by folder name.** When a customer library also
  contains Emerson's standard OOB classes, those are now identified by **matching the tool's
  configured OOB reference library (by name + shape)** and badged `OOB` in the picker — rather
  than trusting an unreliable "From OOB" folder name. Nothing is hidden; it's an informational
  tag so you can tell custom classes from OOB copies at a glance.
- **Renaming a graphic no longer leaves dangling links.** When a converted display gets a
  different name (an automatic `_Conv` rename on a name clash, or mapping onto an existing Live
  graphic), every other graphic that opens it by name is now repointed to the new name. Before,
  the automatic `_Conv` rename fixed only the file itself and left other displays opening the
  old name — a broken call-up that was flagged but not fixed.
- **FHX references to a renamed graphic are now reported.** DeltaV config (a module's
  `PRIMARY_CONTROL_DISPLAY`, `INSTRUMENT_AREA_DISPLAY`, `DETAIL_DISPLAY`, `MMI_PICTURE`) can
  point at a display by name. When a rename changes that name, the conversion now lists exactly
  which module attributes reference the old name (old → new). Op2Live never edits your control
  database — it surfaces the list so you can update them in DeltaV Explorer.

## v19
- **Operate picture scripts are now compiled to TypeScript, not dropped.** Until now, any
  CustomScript VBA that didn't match a known pattern was copied into the Live display as an
  inert `/* VBA ... */` comment — it looked converted, but did nothing at runtime, so
  button logic, conditional show/hide and computed values silently went dead. v19 adds a real
  VBA-to-TypeScript compiler: it parses the handler and emits Live TypeScript that behaves the
  same. Across the validation corpus, **~95% of script handlers now compile clean** (up from
  zero), and the rest are flagged rather than lost.
- **Electronic-signature writes are preserved, never silently bypassed.** In Operate,
  `frsWriteValue` enforced an e-signature prompt on the write. The converted write now goes
  through the DeltaV Live write path with that requirement called out in the Issues board, so

Patina.notes:
# Patina v3

- **Fixed: Batch (and single decompiles) timing out on large binaries.** A
  binary containing one giant basic block — common in obfuscated code, huge
  compiler-generated dispatch routines, or a misidentified code/data region —
  could spin for many minutes in the IR optimizer, blow past the per-target
  batch timeout, and be killed with *no output at all*. The within-block
  inline/dead-code pass was super-linear in the block's size; it's now bounded so
  a single pathological block can no longer stall a run. Such blocks still
  decompile (in the existing flat "fast mode" view) instead of being dropped.
  Example: a 5 MB bridge binary that never finished now completes in ~30s, and a
  whole folder of them decompiles with zero timeouts.

# Patina v2

- **Fixed: Decompile and Batch did nothing but reopen the window.** Clicking
  Decompile (or Batch, or Auto-fix) relaunched the Patina window instead of
  running the decompiler, because the shipped build looked for a separate
  `patina.exe` next to itself and — on Windows' case-insensitive filesystem —
  matched its own `Patina.exe`. Patina is now a single self-contained binary:
  the same exe is both the GUI and the decompiler it runs, so Decompile/Batch/
  Auto-fix work in the installed build.

# Patina v1

- First release inside the CornerBench suite (Reverse Engineering category).
- Decompiles Windows PE and Linux ELF binaries to readable C via an x86
  disassembler (iced-x86) and a custom IR.
- Function navigation with cross-references.

_Edit this when you bump the version; the launcher shows it as "What's new"
after an in-place update._

LiveTools.notes:
# LiveTools v3

A big one. The headline: **the canvas now draws what Live draws.**

## Rendering finally resolves across files

A Live display isn't self-contained — its GEM classes, standard-value colours and
colour tables are the *content of other .ahc files*, usually in other folders.
LiveTools only ever looked in the one "Library folder" box, which was empty by
default. The result: every GEM drew as a grey placeholder tile, and every library
colour was **quietly guessed** by keyword-matching the reference's name. A picture
that was confidently wrong, with nothing to tell you so.

- The renderer now resolves against **every file in your inventory** — add your
  displays *and* your library folders and GEMs expand, with their real colours.
- **GEM instance overrides are applied.** A GEM was previously drawn in its class's
  default colours, ignoring everything the instance changed.
- **Colour tables resolve** to the branch Live takes when no rule matches.
- **Anything still unresolved is reported**, in the status bar, with a tooltip
  listing each reference — including any colour it had to guess. No more silent
  invention.

## Will Graphics Studio actually accept this? — new Import Preflight

A new first tab under **Library** that finds the defects that make a Live import
*fail*, and shows you the exact error Graphics Studio will throw:

- duplicate object names (rejects the **entire display**, not just the object)
- invalid `BooleanCustomPropertyDef` / `NumberCustomPropertyDef` element names
- empty or below-minimum `<Value/>` on int/enum properties
- a colour-returning function bound to a boolean property
- a `String`-typed standard value bound to a localized-string property

Run it before you hand a bundle over.

## Find & Replace you can trust

- **Regex capture groups now work.** `\1` used to be inserted *literally* — regex
  mode was effectively find-only.
- **XML-aware by default.** Edits the parsed document, not raw text, so a broad rule
  can no longer rewrite a tag name, a GUID, or the schema version. Choose what may be
  edited (captions / tags / references / values / names); object names are off by
  default. Untick "XML-aware" for the old raw-text behaviour.
- **It tells you what it *didn't* change.** A new **Skipped** tab lists every match the
  safety gate refused, and why.
- Preview now matches the apply exactly, validation says *what* is wrong instead of
  counting, and you can export a full change-audit CSV.

## Cross-file safety

- **Rename a library item properly** — it moves the file, fixes its name and path,
  and rewrites every reference to it across the project, in one operation.
- **Broken Overrides** report: find GEM overrides that no longer point at anything
  (the breakage a class-side rename causes in every display that uses it) — and
  **repair** the ones that can be unambiguously rebound.
- The editor now **refuses a save that would break other files**, and tells you which.

## Safer, faster, interruptible

- **Every write is atomic and backed up.** A crash mid-save can no longer truncate an
  .ahc, and the original is copied aside before it's overwritten. Saving an unchanged
  file is now a true no-op instead of rewriting its first line.
- **Analyze is ~2.6× faster**, and **Cancel actually works** — on Analyze, on Build,
  on Find & Replace, and on reading a DeltaV backup.
- **One shared file list** across the tabs. Add your project once, not seven times.
- **Translations import to the right string.** Import used to match rows by *position*,
  so adding or deleting any translatable object silently shifted every subsequent
  translation onto the wrong string.
- **SVG export** (vector, crisp at any size) alongside PNG.
- A **headless command line** for CI: `livetools preflight <folder>` exits non-zero if
  a bundle won't import. Also `overrides`, `refs`, `rename`, `fr`, `strings`.

EDSCatalog.notes:
## v2

- Fixed a startup crash (`ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'shared.allenbradley'`). The shared Allen-Bradley EDS library was missing from the packaged build; it is now bundled correctly and the tool launches again.

ABCommission.notes:
## v6

- **Export any table to CSV.** The Discover, Device Explorer and Device Parameters
  tabs each gained an *Export to CSV* button. It writes the table exactly as shown
  — the visible columns, in order, with their heading text — to a CSV file you pick,
  so a discovered-device list, an object-model scan or a full parameter dump can go
  straight into a spreadsheet or a commissioning record. Output is UTF-8 with a BOM
  so Excel opens it without a mojibake prompt.

## v5

- **Probe now prefers the DPI object and reads cleanly on PowerFlex 520-series
  drives.** Validated against a real PowerFlex 525:
  - *Auto* now uses the DPI Parameter Object (0x93) when the device answers a full
    record — it returns name, units, min/max and scaling in one read (e.g.
    parameter 1 → name "Output Freq", units "Hz").
  - The standard Parameter Object (0x0F) fallback was hardened for the PF525's
    quirks: it recovers the parameter name when the drive returns it in the units
    attribute, and **infers the value type from its byte length** when the drive
    reports a non-standard type code — so values show as numbers instead of raw hex.
- Enumeration no longer depends on a working parameter-count read (walks until the
  device stops answering), and the object scan reports parameter-count correctly.

## v4

- **Device introspection — no PDF/EDS needed for most devices.** New *Device
  Explorer* tab plus a *Probe device* button on the parameters tab. The tool now
  asks the device to describe itself:
  - **Identity** — reads the Identity Object (0x01) for product name, revision,
    vendor/type/code, serial.
  - **Self-describing parameters** — walks the DPI Parameter Object (0x93) or the
    standard Parameter Object (0x0F) and reads each parameter's name, type, units,
    min/max/default and **scaling** straight from the device, so you get a live,
    named parameter list (with engineering-unit values) without an EDS. If an EDS
    *is* loaded it enriches the device data (help text, enum strings).
  - **Object-model scan** — probes the well-known CIP classes and shows which the
    device actually implements.
  - **EDS-off-device** (experimental) — uploads the device's own EDS via the File
    Object (0x37), the way Studio 5000/RSLinx do.
  - **Generic explicit-message poker** — send any service/class/instance/attribute
    and decode the reply, for anything the built-in views don't cover.
- **Fixed a second latent read bug:** responses were parsed from the start of the
  raw reply frame instead of the CIP data offset (byte 44). Values/IP config are now
  extracted correctly, and CIP error statuses are decoded to plain English.

## v3

- **Fixed "no parameters could be read" on drives/adapters.** Explicit-message
  reads and writes were being wrapped in an Unconnected_Send routed across a
  backplane to "slot 0" — correct for a ControlLogix chassis, but a standalone
  EtherNet/IP device (PowerFlex drive, EtherNet/IP adapter) has no backplane and
  rejects that route, so every read failed. The tool now sends bare UCMM explicit
  messages directly to the device, the way RSLogix and the drive AOP do.
- **Added the DPI Parameter Object (class 0x93)** used by PowerFlex 520-series
  drives (single read via attr 9; write to NVS via attr 9 or RAM-only via attr 10),
  alongside the generic Parameter Object (0x0F). The Device Parameters tab now has
  a **Parameter object** selector (Auto / DPI 0x93 / Standard 0x0F, default Auto,
  which tries DPI then falls back to 0x0F) and a **Write to** selector (NVS
  permanent / RAM-only temporary).

## v2

- Fixed a startup crash caused by the shared Allen-Bradley EDS library being missing from the packaged build. EDS-based parameter lookup and the tool as a whole now launch correctly.

MP4Transcribe.notes:
# MP4Transcribe v2

- Fixed washed-out text in the status log after switching to the dark theme. The log's
  colour tags were applied once at startup against the light background and never
  re-applied on a theme switch, leaving "muted" entries barely legible on dark.

# MP4Transcribe v1

- First release.
- Offline video/audio transcription with OpenAI Whisper (FFMPEG bundled).
- Batch queue (files or whole folders), real cancellation, and .txt/.srt/.vtt output.
- Language selection + translate-to-English, automatic GPU (CUDA) detection.
- Light/Dark theme; settings and window size are remembered.

_Edit this when you bump the version; the launcher shows it as "What's new"
after an in-place update._

OperateTools.notes:
# OperateTools

## v15
- **New "Bulk table edit" — add / edit / delete rows across many .tgd files at once.**
  The general form of every cross-file tag-group job. Pick an action (Add row, Edit
  column, Delete rows) and, for each column, how its value is computed:
    - **Literal** — the same text in every file (a fixed token name, a description).
    - **Copy** — mirror another column.
    - **Regex extract** — a pattern on a source column with `$1`..`$9` in the template
      (e.g. derive `PACK_1339_MKT_CND` from an existing module row).
    - **Python transform** — a sandboxed Python snippet over the row's columns
      (`token`, `replacement`, `description`, `file`, `stem` are in scope), e.g.
      `replacement.rsplit('_', 1)[0] + '_CND'`. No file or network access; string
      methods only. Return `None` to leave a row unresolved.
  The motivating case — *"reference each unit's new CND module in every graphic so it
  can write the DEPRIORITIZE bit"* — is one **Add row** whose replacement is derived
  per file by a Python snippet (or a regex) from an existing module row. Every change
  is previewed **before → after**; a value that can't be derived (regex miss, or the
  snippet returned `None`) is flagged and **held back from Apply** so it can never be
  written blind. "Skip files that already define this token" makes an Add safe to
  re-run over a fileset that's partly done. Writes honour the same output-folder /
  in-place-backup contract as everything else, and refuse any file that would become
  invalid.
- **New "Tag Groups" tab — edit .tgd tag display groups.** A tag display group is the
  token → tag table iFix substitutes into a picture wherever it finds `@TOKEN@`, and until
  now the only way to touch one was the DeltaV editor, one file at a time. The tab shows a
  group as a plain three-column table (Token, Replacement, Description) and adds the things
  a one-file-at-a-time editor cannot:
    - **Replace across groups** — retag many .tgd files in one pass. Preview lists every
      hit; nothing is written until you Apply.
    - **Cross-check against your pictures** — reads every `@TOKEN@` in the Graphics
      inventory and tells you which tokens are used but not defined (they silently fail to
      substitute) and which are defined but never used. No other tool can do this, because
      it needs to read both the groups and the .grf files.
    - **Errors block a save.** An empty or duplicated token cannot resolve, and a `*_COUNT`
      row whose replacement is not a number drives how many dynamos get drawn — so the tab
      refuses to write a group with either, rather than letting it reach the field.
    - Import / Export CSV for bulk editing in Excel.
  Writing works exactly as it does in Find & Replace: by default you pick an **output
  folder** and your original .tgd files are never touched, or you tick **"Edit in place
  (backup)"** to overwrite them — in which case each original is copied into an
  `.operatetools_backup_<tag>` folder beside it first, and "Undo last write" puts it back.
  Save and Apply stay disabled until you have chosen one of the two.
- **Fixed: saving a tag group no longer leaves deleted rows in the file.** The stock DeltaV
  editor does not shorten a .tgd when a group loses rows, so the deleted content stays in
  the file after the live data — in a sample of 948 production files, 931 carried such
  leftovers, one of them 252 bytes of tokens from an older revision. Those bytes are
  ignored when reading (so a deleted row can never come back) and dropped when saving.

## v14
- **A background task that fails now tells you so.** Previously, if a long-running job
  (Find & Replace preview/apply, "Clean / resync", Script run, bulk render, batch
  visual-diffs, report building) hit an unexpected error, the tool had no way to notice —
  it simply sat on "Previewing…"/"Working…" indefinitely with no message, which was
  impossible to tell apart from a job that was merely slow. The tool now det

CornerCode.notes:
## v8

- The requirements-document generator is now vendored inside CornerCode rather than
  referenced from another project, so it cannot break when that project moves.
- New deterministic helpers: spreadsheet comparison (`sheetdiff`) and API-spec digests
  for Postman/OpenAPI collections (`apispec`). Both do the data work in code rather
  than asking the model to eyeball it.
- More robust loading of very large FHX files: the step that learns how to name
  elements uniquely could take an unreasonable amount of time on unusually wide
  exports. It now uses a bounded search shared with the other FHX tools.

## v6

- **New skill — control narrative → Functional Requirements document.**
  `/control-narrative-to-requirements` turns a control narrative into a
  house-format DeltaV Functional Requirements `.docx`, entirely offline. Load the
  narrative, run the skill, and CornerCode reads it, organises it by system, and
  writes the formatted document — cover page, section numbering, and the standard
  device / custom-logic / interlock / alarm tables — for you to review in Word.

  - **It extracts, it does not invent.** Every system, tag, interlock and alarm
    comes from the narrative. Anything the narrative leaves unstated — a device
    class, an alarm priority, an interlock setpoint — is written as `[TBD]` and
    raised as a numbered **open item** at the back of the document, so the gaps
    are a checklist rather than a guess.

  - **The document is built by the tool, not typed by the model.** Formatting,
    numbering, table layout and validation are deterministic; the model only does
    the reading. It cannot ship a document that is missing a system it found, list
    the same tag twice, or use an alarm priority outside the house set — the
    builder refuses.

  Device classes come back as `[TBD]` — no class library is bundled, so assigning
  classes stays an engineering step. DeltaV migrations only.

VIMTools.notes:
# VIMTools v1

First release. Reverse-engineered toolkit for Emerson/Mynah DeltaV **VIM**
(Virtual I/O Module) configuration files.

- **Open `.vio` configs** — browse the VIM → DeltaV controller → data-set tree,
  with EtherNet/IP device definitions and channel mappings.
- **EtherNet/IP mapping view** — see how assembly buffer bytes map to DeltaV
  signals (`Bytes I4-5 → PCS1-CTL57010101`, etc.).
- **Read-only diagnostics** — connect to a live VIM and observe its status/frames
  (never writes, flashes, or reboots).
- **File validation** — rejects non-`.vio`/corrupt files instead of silently
  showing an empty result.

Clean-room implementation; no Emerson software required.

CornerRDP.notes:
# CornerRDP

## v2
- **New: visual automation builder (Flow tab).** Turn any repetitive RDP chore into a
  saved, repeatable, multi-target automation — a Power-Automate-style sequence of blocks
  (click, type, wait-for / assert element, run command, screenshot, if / repeat /
  for-each-target). No dedicated tool required.
- **Two ways to author:** configure each step in a form, or capture live — click the live
  desktop to drop a Click step at those coordinates, or browse UI elements by name.
- **Built to be reliable:** every step has an error policy (stop / continue / retry N),
  plus wait-for-element / wait-until-settles / assertions, because an RDP screen updates
  asynchronously. Variables ({{output}}, {{exit_code}}, {{target}}, {{index}}, and any you
  save) carry data between steps.
- Flows save as JSON and run against one target or a whole list; each step lights up
  green / red / orange as it runs.

## v1
- First release. A deterministic RDP console for reaching Windows / DeltaV / OT
  nodes that `mstsc` can connect to but WinRM cannot — Standard RDP Security (RC4),
  TLS, and NLA — via the vendored `rdpilot` engine.
- Live desktop with click-to-click control and typing, session clipboard,
  file push/pull, run-as-SYSTEM commands, guest-agent deploy, and reboot.
- Connections are saved as reusable profiles; passwords are never persisted.

DvVMManager.notes:
# DvVMManager

## v4

- **Bulk-import systems from a CSV** — **File ▸ Import systems from CSV…** reads a spreadsheet of
  DeltaV systems and adds them all to your inventory in one go, so you don't have to enter a large
  fleet by hand. Column headers are flexible (e.g. `IP` for host, `role` for station type); only a
  **name** and a **host** are required, everything else is optional. **File ▸ Download CSV template…**
  gives you a ready-to-fill starting point. Passwords are never read from the file — you still enter
  them per session.

## v3

Three big new operations, and the whole tool now speaks plainly.

- **Download setup data** — send your configuration to the DeltaV system without leaving the
  cockpit. Choose *what* to download: **this workstation** (the ProfessionalPlus — use this after
  adding a user), **a specific controller** (picked from a live list read straight off the node),
  or the **whole Control Network**. Leave **Verify only** on to just compare the configuration
  against what's loaded and see the differences — nothing is changed.
- **Add user** — create a DeltaV user in one step: a Windows account with the right DeltaV
  permissions plus the matching DeltaV configuration user. Then download the ProfessionalPlus setup
  data to apply it.
- **Update license** — extend your DeltaV license (the hardware dongle) expiration date. Fetch the
  renewal code automatically from Emerson, or paste one in; a read-only check shows the current
  expiration and never touches the key.
- **Plain language everywhere** — every operation and every field now says what it does, not how it
  works under the hood.

## v2

The UI is now an actual cockpit. v1 was four tabs that each looked like a separate dialog;
v2 is one screen that shows the fleet and the node you're working on at the same time.

- **Node rail** replaces the Inventory *and* Monitoring tabs. Every node is one row with a
  health dot (grey = not probed, green = up, amber = DeltaV stopped, red = unreachable),
  its role glyph, and a padlock while it's still locked. Selecting a node activates it —
  the old "set as active node" step is gone.
- **Node view** replaces the Operations tab's column of list boxes: identity up top, four
  health tiles (reachable / DeltaV services / free disk / license days) where colour
  carries the state, then the operations as cards. Planned operations are shown, dimmed and
  marked "soon", instead of being invisible.
- **Run drawer** slides up when you pick an operation: its parameters, Run / Stop / Open in
  builder, and a live step list where each step names the command it actually runs and goes
  green, amber (retry) or red as it goes. The raw log folds away.
- **Node editor** — the twelve stacked form rows moved off the main screen into a dialog,
  grouped into Connection and DeltaV.
- Yes/no parameters are checkboxes now, not boxes you type "yes" into.

Under the hood this added a drawn line-icon set and a widget kit (cards, stat tiles, status
dots, step rows, chips) to `shared/rdp/ui`, so CornerRDP and future DeltaV tools get the
same look for free.

## v1

First release — a DeltaV VM fleet cockpit built on the CornerBench RDP automation
foundation (the same engine as CornerRDP).

- **Inventory** of DeltaV nodes: RDP connection details plus DeltaV facts (version, role,
  System ID, DeltaV admin account, database export command). Passwords are never saved.
- **Operations** — curated, one-click automations run against the active node with a live
  per-step view, or opened in the flow builder to inspect/adapt:
  - **Export database** — runs the node's DB export command as SYSTEM over SMB, asserts it
    succeeded, and optionally pulls the export back to your PC.
- **Monitoring** — an SMB health grid (reachability, DeltaV services running, free disk)
  for every node you've activated.
- **Builder** — the full flow builder for custom / one-off automation.

More operations (add user, download controllers, update license via the Emerso
